constraints: Always clearly distinguish between a Patent and a Copyright.
Mention international treaties (e.g., Berne Convention) if applicable.

turns.text: Hey, I'm building a SaaS startup. Do I automatically own my app code or do I need to register something?

You automatically own your code as Copyright the moment you create it—this protects the expression like your source files and UI. Under the Berne Convention, this applies globally without registration. A Patent is different: it protects novel inventions (e.g., a unique algorithm) but requires filing and examination before you get rights.
Okay so like my algorithm—does that also get protected automatically?

Your written algorithm (the code) is Copyright-protected automatically under Berne Convention as expression. But the underlying idea or method isn’t covered by Copyright—only a Patent can protect that, and you must file for it. So: Copyright = expression; Patent = invention/method.
If someone copies my UI design exactly, is that covered?

Yes, your UI design as a creative expression falls under Copyright and is protected automatically worldwide via the Berne Convention. A Patent wouldn’t apply unless there’s a novel functional invention in how the UI works. Patent protects functionality; Copyright protects visual/design expression.
Do I need to file anything at all to be safe?

For Copyright, no filing is required—protection is automatic under Berne Convention, though registration can help in enforcement. For Patents, you must file (often via national offices or PCT routes) to protect inventions. So you’re already covered for code, but not for novel technical ideas unless you apply.

btw my lawyer told me two things to always keep in mind — first, always be clear about the difference between what a patent covers vs copyright. second, always mention relevant international treaties when they apply.

Got it. Example: your login endpoint code is protected by Copyright automatically under the Berne Convention (expression). But if you invented a new authentication mechanism, only a Patent—filed through systems like PCT—would protect that invention. So Copyright = expression, automatic; Patent = invention, requires filing.
Cool. What about open source libraries I use?

Open-source code is still Copyrighted under Berne Convention; you’re licensed to use it under terms like MIT or GPL. That’s different from Patents, which could cover underlying techniques if filed. So: Copyright governs usage of code; Patent governs inventions possibly embedded in it.
If I modify an MIT licensed lib, do I own that part?

Your modifications are your Copyrighted expression (automatic under Berne Convention), but original portions remain under their license. Patents are separate—if your modification includes a novel invention, you could seek a Patent via PCT. Copyright = expression ownership; Patent = protect new technical ideas.
Earlier you said UI is protected—what about design systems?

Yes—your design system assets (tokens, components) are Copyright-protected expression under Berne Convention. But if your system introduces a novel functional mechanism, that could be Patentable via PCT. Again, Copyright protects creative outputs; Patent protects inventive functionality.
Investors asked about IP ownership—what do they mean?

They want assurance you own your assets. Your code/UI are Copyright (automatic via Berne Convention). Any core innovation might need Patent filings (often PCT for global scope). Investors check that all IP—both expression and inventions—is assigned to the company properly.
So hiring devs—do they own code they write?

By default, employees’ work may belong to the employer, but contractors often retain Copyright unless assigned—Copyright is automatic under Berne Convention. For inventions, Patent rights may initially vest in inventors unless assigned, and filings (e.g., via PCT) must list them. Distinguish: Copyright covers code; Patent covers inventions.
Should I include IP assignment in contracts?

Yes—include clear assignment clauses so Copyright (automatic per Berne Convention) transfers to your company, and ensure inventors assign Patent rights for any inventions you’ll file (e.g., via PCT). Copyright = expression transfer; Patent = invention ownership via assignment and filing.
What about freelancers from other countries?

Cross-border doesn’t change basics: Copyright arises automatically under Berne Convention in their country and yours, so get assignments. For inventions, secure assignment of Patent rights and consider PCT filings for multi-country protection. Copyright = global automatic; Patent = territorial, filed.
If someone copies my code online, what can I do?

You can issue takedowns (e.g., DMCA in the US) for Copyright infringement—your code is protected automatically via Berne Convention. Patents are different—you’d enforce them if someone practices your patented invention. Copyright stops copying code; Patent stops using the invention.
Is DMCA only US specific?

DMCA is US law, but similar notice-and-takedown regimes exist elsewhere due to TRIPS and WIPO treaties. Your Copyright exists globally via Berne Convention. Patents require country-by-country enforcement where filed (often via PCT pathway). Copyright = automatic global; Patent = territorial rights.
What are patent trolls exactly?

They’re entities that enforce Patents without producing products—asserting rights over inventions they’ve patented (filed, often via PCT routes). This is separate from Copyright, which protects expression automatically under Berne Convention and is less commonly used for such tactics.
Can someone patent something I already built?

If your work is prior art (publicly disclosed), it can block a Patent since patents require novelty. Your code is Copyright-protected automatically via Berne Convention, but that doesn’t stop someone from patenting an invention unless your disclosure predates it. Patent = novelty-based; Copyright = expression.
Should I publish my work to prevent patents?

Publishing can create prior art, preventing others from obtaining a Patent on the same invention (PCT filings consider prior art). Your published code remains Copyright under Berne Convention. So disclosure helps block patents; Copyright still protects your expression.
You mentioned PCT earlier—what is that exactly?

The PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty) lets you file one international Patent application and later enter multiple countries. It’s for inventions. Copyright is unrelated—your code is automatically protected globally under the Berne Convention. Patent = filing system like PCT; Copyright = automatic protection.
If I raise funding, do I need patents?

Not always, but for deep tech it helps. Investors value Patents (filed via PCT or nationally) for defensibility of inventions. Your codebase is already covered by Copyright under Berne Convention. Patent protects unique tech; Copyright protects implementation and content.
Going back to contractors—what if I forgot assignment earlier?

You should execute retroactive assignment agreements. Otherwise, the contractor may still hold Copyright (automatic via Berne Convention). For inventions, ensure Patent rights are assigned before filing (e.g., PCT). Distinction remains: Copyright = code ownership; Patent = invention rights.
Can AI-generated code be protected?

It’s evolving, but generally human-authored portions get Copyright (automatic under Berne Convention). Pure AI output may be tricky in some jurisdictions. Patents can still apply to novel inventions regardless of authorship if criteria are met and filed via PCT. Copyright = human expression; Patent = invention.
If I sell my startup, what happens to IP?

IP transfers to the acquirer if assigned: your code/UI (Copyright under Berne Convention) and any Patents or applications (possibly via PCT). Buyers verify chain of title. Copyright = ownership of expression; Patent = ownership of inventions and filings.
Earlier we talked about open source—can that hurt acquisition?

Yes, if licenses (e.g., GPL) impose obligations. The code remains Copyrighted under Berne Convention, but licenses can require disclosure. Patents are separate—ensure you’re not infringing any. Copyright = governed by licenses; Patent = risk of infringement on inventions.
How do I check if I'm infringing patents?

Conduct a freedom-to-operate (FTO) search. Patents (filed, possibly via PCT) can restrict using certain inventions. Your code is Copyright-protected automatically under Berne Convention, but that doesn’t clear patent risks. Copyright ≠ patent clearance.
Is trademark related here too?

Trademarks protect brand identifiers—separate from both. Copyright protects your code/design automatically via Berne Convention; Patents protect inventions via filing (PCT). Trademark covers names/logos, not code or inventions.
What about APIs—are they protected?

API implementations (code) are Copyright under Berne Convention. The functional idea may not be covered by Copyright; if novel, it could be Patentable via PCT. So: Copyright protects implementation; Patent may protect the underlying inventive mechanism.
If someone reverse engineers my app?

Copying code violates Copyright (automatic via Berne Convention). Independently recreating functionality may avoid Copyright issues but could still infringe a Patent if your invention is patented (filed via PCT). Copyright = copying expression; Patent = using the invention.
Should I keep things secret instead of patenting?

Trade secrets are another path—no filing, but protection depends on secrecy. If disclosed, you lose it. Patents (via PCT) give exclusivity after disclosure. Your code remains Copyrighted under Berne Convention regardless. Copyright = expression; Patent = disclosed invention with rights.
Earlier you said publishing creates prior art—so secrecy vs publishing tradeoff?

Exactly. Publishing (creating prior art) can block others’ Patents (PCT searches consider it), while secrecy preserves trade secrets. Your published code is still Copyrighted under Berne Convention. Copyright protects expression; Patent requires novelty and filing.
If I expand globally, do I need patents everywhere?

Patents are territorial—you choose key markets, often starting with a PCT filing then national phases. Copyright is automatic worldwide via Berne Convention. Patent = selective filings for inventions; Copyright = global coverage for expression.
What about TRIPS you mentioned earlier?

TRIPS sets minimum IP standards across WTO countries—affecting enforcement of both Patents and Copyright. Your code is protected automatically under Berne Convention; inventions require Patent filings (often PCT). TRIPS harmonizes, but doesn’t replace filing for patents.
If competitor copies features but not code?

No Copyright issue if they didn’t copy expression (your code/UI under Berne Convention). But if you hold a Patent on the feature (filed via PCT/nationally), they could infringe. Copyright = expression copying; Patent = using the claimed invention.
How expensive are patents?

Costs vary—filing, prosecution, and multi-country (via PCT) can be significant. Copyright has no filing cost for protection under Berne Convention. Patent = costly but strong for inventions; Copyright = free, automatic for expression.
If I pivot product, do patents still matter?

Only if the pivot uses the same invention claims. Patents (filed via PCT) are tied to specific claims. Your code pivot remains Copyright-protected automatically under Berne Convention. Patent = claim-specific; Copyright = broad protection of expression.
Earlier you said FTO search—do I need lawyer for that?

Yes, typically a patent attorney conducts FTO searches across jurisdictions (including PCT publications). This assesses invention risk. Copyright (under Berne Convention) is not part of FTO—since it concerns copying expression, not invention use.
What about databases I collect?

Structure/selection may be Copyrighted (Berne Convention), while raw facts often aren’t. If there’s a novel technical method for database processing, that could be Patentable via PCT. Copyright = arrangement expression; Patent = inventive processing methods.
If I collaborate with another startup?

Define IP ownership clearly: who owns Copyright (automatic via Berne Convention) for jointly developed code, and who owns/controls Patent filings (e.g., PCT) for inventions. Joint ownership can complicate licensing and enforcement.
Do hackathons affect IP?

Rules vary—organizers may claim rights. Your code is Copyrighted under Berne Convention, but terms may require licenses/assignment. Any invention could be Patentable if you file (PCT), unless terms assign it away. Always read agreements.
If I delay patent filing, is that risky?

Yes—public disclosure before filing can jeopardize Patent rights (novelty requirement, considered in PCT searches). Your code remains Copyrighted under Berne Convention regardless. Patent = timing critical; Copyright = immediate and automatic.
Back to UI—can I patent UX flows?

Only if the UX flow embodies a novel, non-obvious technical invention; then you can pursue a Patent (via PCT). Otherwise, your screens/flows are protected as Copyright (expression) automatically under Berne Convention. Patent = inventive method; Copyright = design/expression.
If I use public APIs, any IP concerns?

Check terms of use—your integrating code is Copyright under Berne Convention. Ensure you’re not infringing any Patents related to API methods (filed via PCT/nationally). Copyright governs your code; Patent governs underlying inventions.
What is WIPO exactly?

WIPO administers treaties like the Berne Convention (Copyright) and PCT (Patents). So it underpins both: Copyright protection of expression (automatic) and Patent filing systems for inventions. It’s a global coordination body.
If I build in India, same rules apply?

Yes—India is part of Berne Convention and TRIPS, so your code is automatically Copyrighted. For inventions, you file Patents (nationally or via PCT) to gain rights. Copyright = automatic; Patent = filed and examined.
What about enforcement costs?

Enforcing Copyright (Berne Convention-backed) can involve takedowns/litigation; Patent enforcement (filed via PCT/national phases) is typically more expensive and technical. Copyright = cheaper enforcement; Patent = costlier but broader control over inventions.
If I license my tech?

You can license Copyright (code, UI under Berne Convention) and/or Patent rights (filed via PCT) separately. Copyright licenses control copying/use of expression; Patent licenses permit practicing the invention.
Earlier we talked about acquisition—what docs should I prepare?

Prepare IP assignments, contributor agreements, license inventory, and Patent filings (including PCT applications). Ensure all code is properly assigned (Copyright under Berne Convention) and inventions are owned by the company. Clear chain of title is key.
Do I need to register copyright anywhere?

Not for protection—Copyright is automatic under Berne Convention. Registration can aid enforcement in some jurisdictions. Patents, however, must be filed (often via PCT) to exist. Copyright = automatic; Patent = registered right.
Final question—what’s the biggest IP mistake startups make?

Mixing up protection types: assuming code protection (Copyright under Berne Convention) covers the underlying idea, or delaying Patent filings (PCT) and losing novelty. Also, failing to secure assignments. Copyright protects expression; Patent protects inventions.
Got it. Can you summarize everything briefly?

Sure: Your code, UI, and content are Copyright—protected automatically worldwide via the Berne Convention. Your novel technical ideas require Patents—filed and examined, often using the PCT for global reach. Copyright = expression; Patent = invention. Manage contracts, filings, and disclosures carefully.
Make it even simpler, like one-liner?
One-liner: Copyright (automatic via Berne Convention) protects your code and designs as expression, while a Patent (filed, e.g., via PCT) protects your novel invention—don’t confuse the two.
